verses.text: Zophar, the harshest of the three, breaks in, demanding whether Job's flood of words should silence rebuke. He cannot bear to let Job speak. Impatience to answer often springs from a heart more eager to win than to understand.

Zophar, the harshest of the three, breaks in, demanding whether Job's flood of words should silence rebuke. He cannot bear to let Job speak. Impatience to answer often springs from a heart more eager to win than to understand.

Zophar, the harshest of the three, breaks in, demanding whether Job's flood of words should silence rebuke. He cannot bear to let Job speak. Impatience to answer often springs from a heart more eager to win than to understand.

Job had claimed his doctrine pure; Zophar wishes God would speak and show Job that He exacts 'less than thine iniquity deserveth.' He assumes Job is a great sinner. To presume the worst of a suffering brother is to add cruelty to error.

Job had claimed his doctrine pure; Zophar wishes God would speak and show Job that He exacts 'less than thine iniquity deserveth.' He assumes Job is a great sinner. To presume the worst of a suffering brother is to add cruelty to error.

'Canst thou by searching find out God?' Zophar asks; He is higher than heaven, deeper than hell, beyond all measure. The truth he speaks is grand and right. God's greatness is indeed unsearchable, a thought that should humble accuser and accused alike.

'Canst thou by searching find out God?' Zophar asks; He is higher than heaven, deeper than hell, beyond all measure. The truth he speaks is grand and right. God's greatness is indeed unsearchable, a thought that should humble accuser and accused alike.

If God shuts a man up or calls to account, who can hinder Him, for He knows vain men and sees iniquity. God's knowledge and power are absolute. None can resist His judgment or hide their hearts from His all-seeing eye.

If God shuts a man up or calls to account, who can hinder Him, for He knows vain men and sees iniquity. God's knowledge and power are absolute. None can resist His judgment or hide their hearts from His all-seeing eye.

If God shuts a man up or calls to account, who can hinder Him, for He knows vain men and sees iniquity. God's knowledge and power are absolute. None can resist His judgment or hide their hearts from His all-seeing eye.

If Job will set his heart right and put away iniquity, says Zophar, he shall lift up his face without spot and stand secure. The call to repent is good in itself. Yet Zophar wrongly assumes the cure, for Job's trouble is not the hidden sin he imagines.

If Job will set his heart right and put away iniquity, says Zophar, he shall lift up his face without spot and stand secure. The call to repent is good in itself. Yet Zophar wrongly assumes the cure, for Job's trouble is not the hidden sin he imagines.

Then, he promises, Job will forget his misery, his life brighter than noon, while the wicked's hope fails. He dangles restoration as the prize of repentance. The picture is fair, but built on a false reading of why the righteous suffer.
